Member-only story

4 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Angular Signals have made state management simpler and more predictable. One of the most practical use cases is automatically saving state so users don’t lose their preferences after refreshing the page.
Instead of manually calling localStorage.setItem() every time a value changes, you can let an effect() watch your signal and synchronize it automatically.
In this article, you’ll learn:

• What effect() does

• Why it’s perfect for persistence

• How to save Signals to localStorage

• How to restore values on application startup

• Multiple real-world examples

• Best practices and common mistakes
Why Use effect()?
An effect() automatically executes whenever any Signal it reads changes.
That means if your Signal stores:

• Theme preference

• Language

• Shopping cart

• Sidebar state

• User settings
the effect can automatically…
